content.text: Domain 1 overview: the physiology map for CT-ICU

Physiology Foundations is the base layer for safe cardiothoracic ICU practice. In CT-ICU, the patient is often unstable because several systems have changed at once: the myocardium may be stunned after cross-clamp and bypass, systemic vascular resistance may be low from post-bypass vasoplegia, filling pressures may be misleading because the ventricles are stiff or externally compressed, pulmonary vascular resistance may be elevated because of hypoxia, hypercapnia, acidosis or high airway pressures, and rhythm may be mechanically ineffective even when the ECG appears organised. Domain 1 gives you the bedside framework to interpret those changes rather than treating isolated monitor numbers.

The central question throughout Domain 1 is: is this patient delivering enough oxygenated blood to the tissues, at an acceptable filling pressure and afterload, without injuring the heart, lungs, kidneys, brain, grafts, valves or surgical repairs? This question is deliberately broader than asking whether the blood pressure is acceptable. A mean arterial pressure of 70 mmHg can mean adequate perfusion if cardiac output is good, lactate is falling, urine output is at least 0.5 mL/kg/hr, skin is warm and SvO2 is acceptable. The same MAP can hide low cardiac output if systemic vascular resistance is high and the patient is cold, oliguric, acidotic and extracting heavily.

The first concept is the cardiac cycle. Each beat has a filling phase, atrial contribution, isovolumetric contraction, ventricular ejection, semilunar valve closure, isovolumetric relaxation and refilling. The ECG triggers this sequence, but pressure gradients open and close valves. The QRS does not prove forward flow. A paced QRS does not prove adequate stroke volume. A sinus rhythm does not prove the ventricles are filling or ejecting effectively. In CT-ICU, the mechanical result of each beat is read through the arterial waveform, pulse pressure, CVP trend, PA catheter data, echo, lactate, urine output and venous oxygen saturation.

The second concept is cardiac output. Cardiac output equals heart rate multiplied by stroke volume. Stroke volume depends on preload, afterload and contractility. Cardiac index is cardiac output corrected for body surface area; a common practical minimum target is at least 2.2 L/min/m2, with many stable adults sitting around 2.5 to 4.0 L/min/m2. Heart rate is not automatically helpful. Tachycardia can compensate for low stroke volume early, but excessive rate shortens diastole, reduces ventricular filling, reduces coronary perfusion time and raises myocardial oxygen demand. Bradycardia after surgery may require pacing if stroke volume cannot compensate.

The third concept is preload. Preload is end-diastolic myocardial stretch, not simply fluid volume. LVEDP, PAWP and echo help estimate left-sided filling. CVP helps estimate right-sided filling pressure, but CVP is not a tank gauge. A high CVP may mean RV failure, high PVR, tamponade, high PEEP, tension pneumothorax, tricuspid regurgitation or venous congestion. A low CVP may occur with hypovolaemia or vasodilation. The key question is whether extra preload will improve stroke volume and whether the patient can tolerate the added filling pressure.

The fourth concept is afterload. LV afterload is the load the LV ejects against and is approximated clinically by systemic vascular resistance, arterial pressure, aortic valve resistance, arterial compliance and wall stress. RV afterload is the load the RV ejects against and is approximated by pulmonary vascular resistance, PA pressures, oxygenation, PaCO2, pH, lung volume and PEEP. Post-bypass vasoplegia is a low-SVR problem. Pulmonary hypertension and RV failure are high-PVR problems. Aortic surgery hypertension is a high-wall-stress problem. These patterns require different responses.

The fifth concept is contractility and inotropy. Contractility is intrinsic myocardial force generation at a given preload and afterload. Inotropy is alteration of that force. After cardiac surgery, poor contractility may reflect myocardial stunning, ischaemia, graft compromise, valve obstruction, acidosis, hypothermia, hypocalcaemia, hyperkalaemia, hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia, inflammatory myocardial depression or RV strain. A weak ventricle that is already adequately filled will not be fixed by repeated fluid; it may need rhythm correction, afterload optimisation, metabolic correction, inotropy, imaging or mechanical support escalation.

The sixth concept is the Frank-Starling mechanism. More filling improves stroke volume only while the ventricle is on the ascending limb of the curve. Once the ventricle reaches the flat limb, fluid mainly raises CVP, PAWP, pulmonary oedema, RV dilation, hepatic congestion and renal venous congestion. Post-cardiac surgery patients often reach the flat limb early because ventricles are stiff, stunned, oedematous, hypertrophied, paced, dyssynchronous or compressed.

The seventh concept is oxygen delivery and consumption. Oxygen delivery depends mainly on cardiac output, haemoglobin and arterial oxygen saturation. The dissolved oxygen contribution from PaO2 is small once haemoglobin is well saturated. A patient can have SpO2 100 percent and still have inadequate oxygen delivery if haemoglobin is 70 g/L or cardiac index is 1.6 L/min/m2. Oxygen consumption rises with fever, shivering, pain, agitation, seizures, respiratory distress and catecholamine excess. During wake-up and extubation, a marginal circulation may fail because oxygen demand suddenly rises.

The final concept is venous oxygen saturation. SvO2 from a pulmonary artery catheter reflects the oxygen left in mixed venous blood after systemic extraction. ScvO2 from a central venous catheter is related but not identical. Low SvO2 usually means oxygen delivery is low, oxygen consumption is high, or both. High SvO2 is not always reassuring; in sepsis, microcirculatory shunting, impaired extraction, hypothermia or deep sedation, venous saturation may be high despite tissue hypoxia. SvO2 must be interpreted with lactate, cardiac output, haemoglobin, oxygenation, temperature, urine output and perfusion.

In Domain 1, no number is interpreted alone. MAP is interpreted with cardiac output and SVR. CVP is interpreted with RV function, ventilation and echo. Pulse pressure is interpreted with arterial line fidelity and stroke volume. SvO2 is interpreted with DO2, VO2 and lactate. The CT-ICU nurse uses physiology to decide what to check first, what deterioration pattern is emerging, and when escalation is urgent.
Why physiology matters in cardiothoracic ICU

A cardiothoracic ICU patient is not simply a generic ICU patient with a sternotomy. The circulation has often been deliberately stopped, cooled, bypassed, reperfused, anticoagulated, reversed, rewarmed and surgically reconstructed. The heart may have been arrested with cardioplegia. The myocardium may be stunned. Systemic vascular tone may be profoundly altered by cardiopulmonary bypass. The patient may have pacing wires, chest drains, a pulmonary artery catheter, vasoactive infusions, residual anaesthesia, coagulopathy, hypothermia, fluid shifts, new valve loading conditions, grafts that need coronary perfusion pressure and a sternum that may hide tamponade until haemodynamics are already deteriorating.

Domain 1 is the mechanism layer beneath everything else. Without it, the nurse sees numbers but not the pattern. With it, the nurse can recognise that falling MAP plus rising CVP plus narrowing pulse pressure after cardiac surgery is not the same problem as falling MAP plus warm peripheries plus wide pulse pressure. The first pattern may be tamponade, RV failure, high PVR or tension physiology. The second may be vasoplegia. Both are hypotension, but the physiology and priorities are different.

The bedside task is not to memorise a static normal range. It is to ask what the heart and circulation are doing right now. Is the rhythm producing mechanical output? Is the ventricle filling? Is the ventricle too full? Can it generate pressure? Can it eject against the current afterload? Is the RV failing because PVR is high? Is the LV underfilled because the RV cannot deliver preload? Is the MAP acceptable only because SVR is high? Is oxygen delivery enough for oxygen demand? Is lactate rising because delivery is failing, demand is high, adrenaline is contributing, or extraction is impaired?
The cardiac cycle connects rhythm to mechanical output

The cardiac cycle is the mechanical sequence of one heartbeat. The ECG shows electrical activation, but valves open and close according to pressure gradients. Atrial systole contributes to ventricular filling. Isovolumetric contraction generates pressure with all valves closed. Ventricular ejection creates stroke volume and the arterial waveform upstroke. Aortic valve closure produces the dicrotic notch. Isovolumetric relaxation allows ventricular pressure to fall until AV valves open and filling begins again.

In CT-ICU, this matters because electrical and mechanical success can diverge. A patient can have ventricular pacing spikes and broad QRS complexes but poor stroke volume. A patient can be in sinus rhythm but have severe tamponade or RV failure and therefore inadequate filling. A patient can have rapid AF and lose atrial kick, which may be catastrophic in a stiff hypertrophied LV after aortic valve surgery. A patient can have bradycardia after surgery and need temporary pacing because stroke volume cannot rise enough to preserve cardiac output.

For post-CABG patients, diastole is especially important because coronary perfusion occurs predominantly during diastole. Tachycardia shortens diastole, increases myocardial oxygen demand and may reduce graft and native coronary perfusion reserve. For valve patients, atrial contribution and ventricular compliance matter because pre-operative pressure or volume loading may have remodelled the ventricle. For TAVI patients, conduction disturbance and heart block can alter mechanical timing. For aortic surgery patients, hypertension during systole increases wall stress on repairs and residual aorta.

content.title: Cardiac cycle pressure-gradient map
content.description: A flowchart showing electrical activation leading to pressure changes, valve opening and closure, ejection, relaxation and refilling. This diagram helps the nurse connect ECG timing to arterial waveform timing and mechanical output.
elements: Aortic valve closure: dicrotic notch and S2
AV valve opening and ventricular filling
teachingPoints: Electrical activity starts the cycle but pressure gradients move the valves.
A paced rhythm or organised ECG does not prove adequate stroke volume.
The arterial line waveform is a mechanical trace of LV ejection and aortic valve closure.

content.text: Cardiac output connects the cardiac cycle to organ perfusion

Cardiac output is the volume of blood ejected per minute. The formula is CO equals heart rate multiplied by stroke volume. Cardiac index corrects this value for body surface area. A common minimum adult target in low-output assessment is at least 2.2 L/min/m2, although a sedated patient with low demand may tolerate this better than a febrile, shivering or anaemic patient. Stable adults are often around 2.5 to 4.0 L/min/m2.

Stroke volume is the volume ejected per beat. It depends on preload, afterload and contractility. Heart rate determines how often that stroke volume is delivered. The problem is that heart rate and stroke volume are not independent. A rising heart rate can compensate for low stroke volume at first. Beyond a point, tachycardia shortens diastolic filling, lowers stroke volume, reduces coronary perfusion time and increases oxygen demand. The total cardiac output can fall despite a faster rate.

This is why post-operative AF at 140 to 160/min can destabilise a fresh valve or CABG patient. The ventricle loses atrial contribution, diastole shortens, myocardial oxygen demand rises and stroke volume falls. Conversely, bradycardia at 40 to 50/min may be poorly tolerated after cardiac surgery if stroke volume cannot increase enough to maintain cardiac output. Temporary pacing is therefore not only rhythm management; it is cardiac output management.
Preload is filling and stretch, not simply fluid volume

Preload is end-diastolic myocardial fibre stretch. Left-sided preload is approximated by LVEDV, LVEDP, PAWP or echo assessment. Right-sided preload is often approximated by CVP or right atrial pressure. These are estimates and pressures, not direct measurements of total circulating volume. A ventricle with poor compliance can have a high filling pressure at modest volume. A vasodilated patient can have reduced effective venous return despite having large total body water.

CVP is especially prone to misinterpretation. A CVP of 14 mmHg can be caused by RV failure, high PVR, tamponade, high PEEP, tension pneumothorax, venous congestion or tricuspid regurgitation. It does not automatically mean the patient is overfilled. A CVP of 4 mmHg can suggest low right-sided filling pressure, but fluid should still be judged by whether stroke volume increases and whether the patient can tolerate added volume.

Positive pressure ventilation changes preload interpretation because intrathoracic pressure is transmitted to the great veins and heart. PEEP can increase measured CVP while reducing venous return and changing transmural filling pressure. In RV failure, the CVP may be high because the RV cannot eject forward; the LV may still be underfilled because blood is not crossing the pulmonary circulation effectively.

A falling MAP with rising CVP and narrowing pulse pressure after cardiac surgery is an escalation pattern. Consider tamponade, RV failure, high PVR, tension pneumothorax, excessive PEEP or severe congestion. Do not treat this pattern as simple hypovolaemia without urgent senior review and mechanical assessment.
Afterload separates LV problems from RV problems

Afterload is the load against which a ventricle ejects. LV afterload is related to systemic vascular resistance, arterial pressure, aortic valve resistance, arterial compliance and wall stress. RV afterload is related to pulmonary vascular resistance, pulmonary artery pressure, oxygenation, PaCO2, pH, PEEP, lung volume and pulmonary vascular tone.

Systemic vascular resistance is commonly around 800 to 1200 dyn s/cm5. Pulmonary vascular resistance is usually below 2 Wood units or approximately 24 to 160 dyn s/cm5. The LV is designed to eject into a high-pressure systemic circuit. The RV is designed to eject into a low-pressure pulmonary circuit. Therefore, an acute rise in PVR can cause RV dilation, tricuspid regurgitation, septal shift, reduced LV filling, low cardiac output, rising CVP and hypotension.

Post-bypass vasoplegia is a low-SVR state. The patient may be hypotensive, warm, vasodilated and have a wide pulse pressure with normal or high cardiac output. This differs from cold low-output shock, where cardiac index is low and SVR may be high. Aortic surgery creates a different afterload concern: hypertension increases wall stress on the repair and residual aorta. RV failure creates another afterload concern: hypoxia, hypercapnia, acidosis and high PEEP may all worsen PVR and collapse forward flow.
Contractility and inotropy explain pump failure

Contractility is intrinsic myocardial force generation at a given preload and afterload. Inotropy is alteration of that force. After cardiopulmonary bypass, contractility may be impaired by myocardial stunning, ischaemia-reperfusion injury, cardioplegia, hypothermia, acidosis, electrolyte disturbance, inflammatory myocardial depression, graft compromise, valve dysfunction or RV strain.

Ejection fraction is useful but imperfect because it is load-dependent. A small underfilled LV may look hyperdynamic while ejecting a low absolute stroke volume. A vasodilated patient may appear to have a strong EF because afterload is low. A high-SVR patient may look worse because the ventricle cannot eject effectively against resistance. Contractility must therefore be interpreted with chamber size, stroke volume, cardiac index, filling pressures, afterload and perfusion markers.

Inotropes such as adrenaline, dobutamine, milrinone and levosimendan conceptually improve contractile performance through different pathways, but none should be thought of as generic blood pressure drugs. Adrenaline can increase inotropy and heart rate but can raise lactate and arrhythmia risk. Dobutamine improves beta-mediated inotropy but may reduce SVR. Milrinone improves inotropy and reduces PVR/SVR but can cause hypotension and accumulates with renal dysfunction. Levosimendan increases calcium sensitivity and may be used in selected severe ventricular dysfunction contexts depending on local availability and practice.
Frank-Starling explains why fluid helps some patients and harms others

The Frank-Starling mechanism links preload to stroke volume. On the ascending limb of the curve, more preload increases myocardial fibre stretch and improves stroke volume. On the flat limb, more preload mostly increases filling pressure without improving flow. In CT-ICU, this distinction is crucial because many post-operative hearts are stiff, oedematous, stunned, hypertrophied, paced or compressed.

Fluid responsiveness means stroke volume or cardiac output increases after a preload challenge, often by 10 to 15 percent depending on the method used. Fluid tolerance means the patient can safely receive the fluid without harmful congestion. A patient can be fluid responsive but not fluid tolerant. This is common in RV failure or stiff LV physiology, where added volume may raise CVP, PAWP, pulmonary oedema, RV dilation and renal venous congestion.
Oxygen delivery and consumption define whether the physiology is adequate

Oxygen delivery is calculated as cardiac output multiplied by arterial oxygen content. Arterial oxygen content depends mainly on haemoglobin and arterial oxygen saturation. The formula is CaO2 equals 1.34 multiplied by haemoglobin multiplied by SaO2, plus 0.003 multiplied by PaO2. The dissolved oxygen component from PaO2 is small once saturation is high. This is why SpO2 100 percent does not guarantee oxygen delivery if haemoglobin or cardiac output is low.

Oxygen consumption is the oxygen used by tissues per minute. Resting adult VO2 is roughly 200 to 250 mL/min, but it rises with fever, shivering, pain, agitation, seizures, sepsis, work of breathing and catecholamine excess. A post-cardiac surgery patient can appear stable while sedated and ventilated, then deteriorate during wake-up or extubation because VO2 rises and the heart cannot increase delivery to match demand.
Venous oxygen saturation shows the downstream balance

SvO2 is oxygen saturation in pulmonary artery blood after venous return from the whole body has mixed. It reflects the relationship between oxygen delivery and oxygen consumption. A usual range is about 60 to 80 percent, and many CT-ICU targets use at least 65 to 70 percent if the rest of the clinical picture agrees. ScvO2 is central venous oxygen saturation from the SVC or right atrial region. It is easier to obtain but is not identical to SvO2.

Low SvO2 usually means oxygen extraction is increased because delivery is low, demand is high or both. Common causes include low cardiac output, anaemia, hypoxaemia, shivering, fever, pain, agitation, seizures and respiratory distress. High SvO2 is not automatically reassuring. It may mean delivery is high and demand is low, but it may also mean impaired extraction, microcirculatory shunting, sepsis, mitochondrial dysfunction, hypothermia, deep sedation or sampling error.

content.title: Domain 1 bedside interpretation sequence
items: Confirm the number is real. Check arterial line level and damping, CVP or PA catheter waveform, sampling source, ECG rhythm, pulse oximetry waveform and recent transducer zeroing.
Separate pressure from flow. Ask whether MAP is low because cardiac output is low, SVR is low, or both. Ask whether MAP is normal only because SVR is high.
Assess rhythm and mechanical timing. Look for AF, bradycardia, heart block, pacing capture, loss of AV synchrony and excessive rate.
Assess preload and congestion together. Use CVP, PAWP, pulse pressure, echo, drains, oxygenation, lung mechanics, fluid balance and response to fluid.
Assess afterload separately for LV and RV. Use SVR, PVR, PA pressures, MAP, diastolic pressure, oxygenation, PaCO2, pH and ventilator settings.
Assess contractility. Use echo, CI, pulse pressure, lactate, SvO2, vasoactive requirement, ST changes, temperature and electrolytes.
Assess oxygen economy. Review cardiac output, haemoglobin, SaO2, PaO2, lactate, SvO2 or ScvO2, temperature, shivering, work of breathing and pain.
Escalate early when a dangerous pattern appears, especially low MAP with rising CVP, narrowing pulse pressure, rising lactate, falling urine output or new rhythm deterioration after cardiac surgery.

content.text: Clinical Pearls

content.title: Pearl 1: pressure is not perfusion
content.text: MAP is necessary, but it is not the same as cardiac output or oxygen delivery. A patient with MAP 70 mmHg, CI 3.0 L/min/m2, falling lactate, warm skin and urine output above 0.5 mL/kg/hr is very different from a patient with MAP 70 mmHg on high noradrenaline, CI 1.6 L/min/m2, rising lactate, low SvO2 and cold peripheries. The second patient has pressure support, not adequate flow.

content.title: Pearl 2: the ECG is electrical; the arterial line is mechanical
content.text: An organised ECG rhythm does not prove effective ejection. The arterial line upstroke, pulse pressure and dicrotic notch provide mechanical information about LV ejection and aortic valve closure. A paced rhythm can be electrically captured but haemodynamically inadequate if rate, AV timing, ventricular synchrony, preload, afterload or contractility are poor.

content.title: Pearl 3: CVP is a congestion clue, not a fluid instruction
content.text: CVP must be interpreted with RV function, PVR, ventilation, PEEP, intrathoracic pressure, tricuspid valve function and pericardial pressure. A high CVP with shock after cardiac surgery is dangerous until proven otherwise. It may indicate tamponade, RV failure, high PVR, tension pneumothorax, high PEEP or severe venous congestion.

content.title: Pearl 4: the RV fails differently from the LV
content.text: The LV often fails backward into the lungs and forward into low systemic output. The RV fails into the venous system and underfills the LV. RV failure commonly shows rising CVP, hepatic congestion, renal venous congestion, RV dilation, septal shift, low CI and hypotension. Hypoxia, hypercapnia, acidosis and high PEEP are haemodynamic threats because they raise PVR.

content.title: Pearl 5: post-bypass vasoplegia is not the same as low-output shock
content.text: Vasoplegia is a low-SVR distributive state after bypass. The patient may be warm, hypotensive, vasodilated, have low diastolic pressure, wide pulse pressure and normal or high cardiac output. Cold low-output shock has poor flow, often high SVR, low pulse pressure, rising lactate and falling SvO2. Treating both patterns the same way can harm the patient.

content.title: Pearl 6: fluid responsiveness is not fluid tolerance
content.text: A patient may increase stroke volume after fluid but still be harmed by the added volume. This matters in RV failure, stiff LV physiology, pulmonary hypertension and post-bypass myocardial oedema. A good response is improved flow with acceptable pressure rise. A bad response is rising CVP or PAWP without better cardiac output, worsening oxygenation or increasing RV dilation.

content.title: Pearl 7: SpO2 is not oxygen delivery
content.text: Oxygen delivery depends on cardiac output, haemoglobin and saturation. A patient can have SpO2 100 percent but poor delivery if CI is 1.7 L/min/m2 or haemoglobin is 70 g/L. When lactate rises and SvO2 falls despite good SpO2, look at flow, haemoglobin and oxygen demand.

content.title: Pearl 8: high SvO2 can be bad
content.text: A high SvO2 may mean adequate delivery and low demand, but it may also mean impaired extraction. In sepsis, inflammatory shock, microcirculatory shunting, mitochondrial dysfunction, hypothermia or deep sedation, venous blood may return with high saturation while tissues are still hypoxic. High SvO2 plus rising lactate is not reassuring.

content.title: Pearl 9: post-operative deterioration should be translated into a physiology pattern
content.text: Do not label a patient as simply hypotensive. Decide whether the pattern is vasoplegic, hypovolaemic, tamponade-like, RV failure, LV pump failure, high-afterload, rhythm-related, oxygen-delivery failure or mixed. The right escalation depends on the pattern.
Equipment Tie-In

Domain 1 physiology becomes visible through CT-ICU equipment. The monitor is not just displaying numbers; it is displaying pressure, flow surrogates, rhythm timing, oxygenation, ventilation, filling pressure, extraction and device-patient interaction. The nurse must know what each device actually measures and what it only implies.
Arterial line

The arterial line measures pressure in an artery. It does not directly measure cardiac output. The upstroke reflects LV ejection, the pulse pressure reflects stroke volume and arterial compliance, the dicrotic notch reflects aortic valve closure, and the diastolic pressure contributes to coronary perfusion. A narrow pulse pressure can suggest low stroke volume, but overdamping can mimic this. A high systolic pressure with low diastolic pressure may suggest underdamping or vasoplegia depending on waveform and clinical context.

content.title: Arterial waveform physiology
rows: Slow upstroke
Low contractility, low stroke volume, aortic stenosis physiology or excessive afterload
Check damping, transducer level, tubing, flush test and NIBP correlation
Assess CI, echo, lactate, SvO2 and valve history
Narrow pulse pressure
Low stroke volume from hypovolaemia, tamponade, LV failure, RV failure or high afterload
Exclude overdamping
Check drains, CVP trend, echo, lactate and urine output
Wide pulse pressure
Vasoplegia, aortic regurgitation, high output or underdamping
Exclude underdamping
Assess SVR, warmth, diastolic pressure and valve status
Dicrotic notch absent
Low flow, damping, low SVR or waveform artefact
Perform square-wave/fast-flush assessment according to local practice
Do not make IABP timing or haemodynamic conclusions until trace quality is known

content.text: CVP monitoring

CVP monitoring gives right-sided pressure information. It is useful for trends, waveform interpretation and identifying venous congestion patterns. It is not a direct measure of circulating volume. CVP must be levelled, zeroed and interpreted with ventilation, PEEP, RV function and PVR. A rising CVP with falling output is more concerning than a stable CVP value outside a textbook range.
Pulmonary artery catheter

The pulmonary artery catheter connects multiple Domain 1 concepts in one device. It can provide CVP, PA pressures, PAWP, cardiac output, cardiac index, SVR, PVR and SvO2. This allows separation of preload, afterload, flow and oxygen extraction. It is valuable when used correctly, but the numbers are vulnerable to waveform error, wedging error, catheter migration, arrhythmias, severe tricuspid regurgitation, intracardiac shunts and thermodilution technique problems.
Echo

Echo is the fastest way to connect numbers to anatomy and movement. It can show LV size and systolic function, RV dilation, septal shift, valve lesions, tamponade, LV underfilling, RV pressure overload and gross volume state. When the arterial line, CVP and clinical picture do not agree, echo often clarifies the mechanism.
Ventilator

The ventilator is a haemodynamic device as well as a respiratory device. PEEP and positive pressure ventilation affect venous return, RV afterload and measured filling pressures. Hypoxia raises PVR. Hypercapnia and acidosis raise PVR and can worsen RV failure. Excessive lung volume can compress pulmonary vessels and reduce venous return. In RV-risk patients, ventilator changes can produce immediate haemodynamic consequences.
Pacing box

Temporary pacing changes heart rate, atrial contribution, AV synchrony and ventricular activation. AAI, VVI and DDD modes have different haemodynamic consequences. VVI pacing can maintain rate but lose atrial contribution. DDD pacing can restore AV synchrony if sensing and capture are correct. Rate must be high enough to support CO but not so high that diastolic filling and coronary perfusion are compromised.
IABP

The intra-aortic balloon pump is timed to the cardiac cycle. Inflation after aortic valve closure augments diastolic pressure and coronary perfusion. Deflation just before systole reduces LV afterload. Early inflation can oppose LV ejection. Late inflation loses augmentation. Late deflation increases afterload. IABP interpretation therefore requires understanding systole, diastole and the dicrotic notch.

content.title: Monitor-to-physiology interpretation map
content.description: A comparison diagram linking common CT-ICU devices to the physiology they measure or infer.
elements: Arterial line: pressure, waveform, pulse pressure, dicrotic notch
CVP: right-sided pressure and venous congestion clue
PA catheter: PA pressures, PAWP, cardiac output, SVR, PVR, SvO2
Echo: chamber size, contractility, RV dilation, septal shift, tamponade
Pacing box: rate, AV synchrony, capture and mechanical timing
IABP: diastolic augmentation and afterload reduction timing
teachingPoints: Every device measures some physiology directly and infers other physiology indirectly.
When numbers conflict, check the measurement first and then use physiology to decide which pattern fits.
The same MAP may mean different things depending on cardiac output, SVR and clinical perfusion.

content.text: Targets and Numbers

Domain 1 targets are not rigid universal goals. They are interpretation anchors. The correct target depends on operation type, ventricular function, bleeding risk, neurological risk, chronic hypertension, pulmonary hypertension, RV function, valve pathology and phase of recovery. However, real numbers matter because vague terms such as adequate, normal or stable are not safe enough at the bedside.

content.title: Core adult CT-ICU physiology targets
rows: MAP
Commonly 65 to 85 mmHg
Systemic perfusion pressure and coronary perfusion support
MAP below 65 with rising lactate, low urine output or falling SvO2; MAP too high after aortic surgery or active bleeding
Cardiac index
At least 2.2 L/min/m2; often 2.5 to 4.0 L/min/m2 when stable
Forward flow indexed to body size
CI below 2.2 with poor perfusion markers suggests low-output physiology
Urine output
At least 0.5 mL/kg/hr
Practical renal perfusion and congestion marker
Falling urine output with low CI, rising CVP or rising lactate
Lactate
Less than 2 mmol/L or clearly falling
Marker of oxygen delivery-demand mismatch or metabolic stress
Rising or non-clearing lactate after initial resuscitation
SvO2
Usually 60 to 80 percent; often target at least 65 to 70 percent if context agrees
Global oxygen extraction marker
Low SvO2 with rising lactate; high SvO2 with rising lactate and shock
ScvO2
Often above 70 percent, but not identical to SvO2
Central venous oxygen balance estimate
Falling trend or mismatch with lactate/perfusion
CVP
Often 5 to 12 mmHg, highly context-dependent
Right-sided pressure and congestion clue
Rising CVP with falling MAP, narrowing pulse pressure or oliguria
PAWP/PCWP
Often 8 to 15 mmHg if waveform is valid
Left-sided filling pressure estimate
High PAWP with hypoxaemia or pulmonary oedema; low PAWP with low output and underfilling pattern
SVR
Approximately 800 to 1200 dyn s/cm5
LV/systemic vascular load
Low SVR with warm hypotension; high SVR with low CI and cold shock
PVR
Usually below 2 Wood units or 24 to 160 dyn s/cm5
RV/pulmonary vascular load
High PVR with rising CVP, RV dilation, low CI or hypoxaemia
Potassium
Often 4.0 to 4.5 mmol/L in many post-cardiac surgery practices
Electrical stability and pacing/arrhythmia risk
Hypokalaemia, hyperkalaemia, ventricular ectopy, AF or pacing instability
Magnesium
Often at least 0.8 to 1.0 mmol/L depending local units and policy
Electrical stability and AF/VT risk reduction
Low magnesium with ectopy, AF, torsades risk or refractory hypokalaemia
Ionised calcium
Often around 1.1 to 1.3 mmol/L
Contractility, vascular tone and coagulation
Low ionised calcium with hypotension, poor contractility or massive transfusion

content.title: Operation-specific emphasis
rows: Post-CABG
Maintain coronary and graft perfusion while avoiding tachycardia and excessive afterload
Avoid sustained tachycardia; protect diastolic pressure; assess ST changes, pulse pressure, lactate and urine output
Being reassured by MAP while CI and SvO2 fall
Post-valve surgery
Match preload and rhythm to altered ventricular compliance and new valve loading
Preserve sinus rhythm where important; avoid severe hypertension; watch pulmonary oedema and filling pressures
Underestimating the haemodynamic effect of new AF
TAVI
Watch conduction disturbance, heart block, vascular complications and LV loading changes
Assess rhythm, PR/QRS changes, pacing need, BP, vascular access bleeding and neurological status
Assuming a stable ECG earlier excludes later conduction deterioration
Aortic surgery
Protect the repair and residual aorta from excessive pressure and shear
Avoid hypertension while maintaining organ perfusion; monitor neurological, renal and bleeding markers
Treating hypertension as benign because urine output is adequate
RV failure or pulmonary hypertension
Reduce PVR and maintain RV coronary perfusion
Avoid hypoxia, hypercapnia, acidosis and excessive PEEP; watch CVP, PA pressures, RV echo and CI
Giving repeated fluid for hypotension while the RV dilates further
Vasoplegia after bypass
Restore vascular tone while preserving flow
Look for warm hypotension, low diastolic pressure, wide pulse pressure, low SVR and normal/high CI
Confusing vasoplegia with hypovolaemic low-output shock
Tamponade
Recognise impaired filling and escalate early
Watch rising CVP, falling MAP, narrowing pulse pressure, low drain output from clot, oliguria and lactate rise
Waiting for classic Beck triad in a ventilated post-operative patient
Cardiogenic shock
Restore forward flow and oxygen delivery
Use CI, lactate, SvO2, urine output, echo, filling pressures and vasoactive dose trend
Treating pressure alone while afterload worsens low output

content.title: Escalation triggers using Domain 1 physiology
items: Escalate urgently for falling MAP with rising CVP, narrowing pulse pressure, falling urine output or rising lactate after cardiac surgery.
Escalate urgently for CI below 2.2 L/min/m2 with rising lactate, low SvO2, oliguria or cold peripheries.
Escalate urgently for new rapid AF, high-grade AV block, severe bradycardia or pacing failure with haemodynamic compromise.
Escalate urgently for rising PA pressures, rising CVP, RV dilation, hypoxaemia, hypercapnia or acidosis in an RV-risk patient.
Escalate urgently for high or rising lactate that does not match apparently acceptable MAP or SpO2.
Escalate urgently when a number does not fit the patient after equipment error has been excluded.

content.text: Targets are useful only when interpreted as a pattern. A MAP target without cardiac output can hide shock. A CVP target without RV and ventilator context can mislead. An SvO2 target without lactate and extraction context can falsely reassure. A urine output target without venous congestion context can miss RV failure.

quiz.question: Which statement best describes why Domain 1 physiology matters in CT-ICU?
options: It helps interpret whether monitor numbers represent adequate flow, filling, resistance, contractility and oxygen delivery.
It replaces the need for arterial line waveform assessment.
It proves that MAP alone is enough to judge perfusion.
It applies only to patients with pulmonary artery catheters.
quiz.explanation: Domain 1 physiology provides the mechanism behind monitor data. CT-ICU deterioration is often a pattern involving pressure, flow, filling, resistance, rhythm, contractility and oxygen delivery. MAP alone cannot identify whether the patient has adequate cardiac output, excessive SVR, vasoplegia, tamponade, RV failure or oxygen delivery failure.

quiz.question: What is the basic formula for cardiac output?
options: Cardiac output equals heart rate multiplied by stroke volume.
Cardiac output equals MAP multiplied by CVP.
Cardiac output equals haemoglobin multiplied by SpO2.
Cardiac output equals SVR divided by PVR.
quiz.explanation: Cardiac output equals heart rate multiplied by stroke volume. Stroke volume is determined by preload, afterload and contractility. Heart rate can compensate for low stroke volume only up to the point where diastolic filling and coronary perfusion become compromised.

quiz.question: A fresh post-cardiac surgery patient has MAP 70 mmHg on escalating noradrenaline, CI 1.6 L/min/m2, cold peripheries, urine output 10 mL/hr, rising lactate and SvO2 52 percent. What is the best interpretation?
options: The patient has acceptable pressure but inadequate forward flow and oxygen delivery.
The patient is stable because MAP is above 65 mmHg.
The patient has isolated hypoxaemia.
The patient is definitely fluid overloaded.
quiz.explanation: The MAP is being supported, but the cardiac index, lactate, SvO2, urine output and peripheral perfusion show inadequate forward flow and oxygen delivery. This is the classic reason pressure must be separated from flow. The next step is mechanism assessment: preload, rhythm, contractility, afterload, RV function, tamponade and oxygen demand.

quiz.question: A patient after valve surgery develops rapid AF at 150/min with falling MAP, narrowing pulse pressure and falling urine output. Which Domain 1 mechanism is most likely contributing?
options: Loss of atrial kick and shortened diastolic filling time reducing stroke volume.
Improved ventricular filling from irregular atrial activity.
Reduced myocardial oxygen demand from tachycardia.
Direct correction of LV afterload by AF.
quiz.explanation: Rapid AF removes coordinated atrial systole and shortens diastole. In a stiff post-valve ventricle, atrial contribution may be important for filling. Reduced filling time and increased oxygen demand can lower stroke volume, pulse pressure, MAP and urine output.

quiz.question: Why can a high CVP with hypotension after cardiac surgery be more concerning than a low CVP?
options: It may indicate obstructed filling, RV failure, high PVR, tamponade, high PEEP or tension physiology rather than simple preload deficiency.
It always proves the patient needs a large fluid bolus.
It proves the arterial line is underdamped.
It means cardiac output is always high.
quiz.explanation: A high or rising CVP with hypotension indicates high right-sided pressure with poor forward flow or impaired filling. After cardiac surgery, this can reflect tamponade, RV failure, pulmonary hypertension, high PEEP, tension pneumothorax or severe venous congestion. Repeated fluid can worsen RV dilation or congestion if the problem is not preload deficiency.

quiz.question: A patient has SpO2 100 percent, haemoglobin 72 g/L, CI 1.8 L/min/m2, rising lactate and low SvO2. What is the most accurate interpretation?
options: Oxygen delivery is likely inadequate despite normal oxygen saturation.
Oxygen delivery is guaranteed because SpO2 is 100 percent.
The low haemoglobin is irrelevant if PaO2 is high.
SvO2 cannot fall when SpO2 is normal.
quiz.explanation: Oxygen delivery depends mainly on cardiac output, haemoglobin and arterial saturation. SpO2 only tells saturation. This patient has both low haemoglobin and low cardiac index, so arterial oxygen content and delivery are likely inadequate. Rising lactate and low SvO2 support oxygen delivery-demand mismatch.

diagrams.title: Domain 1 bedside physiology map
diagrams.description: A central map showing how cardiac cycle, cardiac output, preload, afterload, contractility, Frank-Starling physiology, oxygen delivery and venous saturation connect at the bedside.
teachingPoints: Cardiac output is generated by heart rate and stroke volume.
Stroke volume is shaped by preload, afterload and contractility.
Oxygen delivery fails when cardiac output, haemoglobin or saturation are inadequate for oxygen demand.
Venous saturation and lactate show whether the downstream oxygen economy is under strain.

diagrams.title: Monitor-to-physiology interpretation map
diagrams.description: A comparison diagram linking CT-ICU devices to the physiology they measure or infer.
elements: Arterial line: MAP, pulse pressure, waveform quality, dicrotic notch
CVP: right-sided filling pressure and venous congestion clue
PA catheter: PA pressures, PAWP, CO, CI, SVR, PVR, SvO2
Echo: chamber size, function, valves, RV dilation, septal shift, tamponade
Ventilator: oxygenation, PaCO2, pH, PEEP, venous return and PVR
Pacing box: rate, capture, sensing and AV synchrony
IABP: diastolic augmentation and systolic afterload reduction
teachingPoints: Each device measures only part of the physiology.
Conflicting data require equipment validation before interpretation.
The best interpretation combines pressure, flow, rhythm, filling, resistance and oxygen economy.

diagrams.title: Post-cardiac surgery instability pattern algorithm
diagrams.description: A bedside algorithm that starts with hypotension or rising lactate and separates vasoplegia, low-output shock, tamponade, RV failure, hypovolaemia and oxygen-demand states.
elements: Start: falling MAP, rising lactate, oliguria or falling SvO2
Assess pulse pressure and arterial waveform
Assess temperature, haemoglobin, oxygenation and ventilation
Use echo for chamber size, RV function, LV function and tamponade
Classify as vasoplegia, hypovolaemia, tamponade, RV failure, LV pump failure, rhythm problem or oxygen delivery-demand mismatch
teachingPoints: The same blood pressure can represent different shock mechanisms.
Rising CVP with falling output is a high-risk post-cardiac surgery pattern.
Echo and haemodynamic trends are often needed to confirm the mechanism.

references.title: Physiology, Cardiac Output
references.supports: Defines cardiac output as heart rate multiplied by stroke volume and explains preload, afterload and contractility as stroke volume determinants.

references.title: Physiology, Cardiovascular
references.supports: Supports the integrated cardiovascular physiology framework including cardiac output, stroke volume, preload, Frank-Starling law, afterload and ejection fraction.

references.sourceName: Journal of Cardiothoracic and Vascular Anesthesia / PMC
references.title: Vasoplegia after cardiopulmonary bypass: A narrative review
references.supports: Supports post-cardiopulmonary bypass vasoplegia as a clinically important low-SVR syndrome after cardiac surgery.

references.title: Vasoplegic Syndrome after Cardiopulmonary Bypass in Cardiovascular Surgery
references.supports: Supports vasoplegic syndrome incidence, risk factors and distributive shock physiology after cardiopulmonary bypass.

references.title: Clinical review: use of venous oxygen saturations as a goal
references.supports: Supports venous oxygen saturation as a marker of the balance between oxygen delivery and oxygen demand.

references.title: Central and Mixed Venous O2 Saturation
references.supports: Supports interpretation of SvO2 and ScvO2, including their physiological basis and limitations in critical illness.

references.title: Definitions of low cardiac output syndrome after cardiac surgery and their effect on incidence, treatment and outcome
references.supports: Supports LCOS after cardiac surgery as inadequate cardiac pump function causing reduced oxygen delivery and tissue hypoxia.

references.sourceName: Journal of Clinical Monitoring and Computing / PMC
references.title: Hemodynamic monitoring strategies in cardiac surgery
references.supports: Supports the role of haemodynamic monitoring strategies in cardiac surgery, including cardiac index, stroke volume variation and perfusion markers.

references.title: Contemporary Review of Hemodynamic Monitoring in Circulatory Shock
references.supports: Supports haemodynamic assessment as an adjunct to physical examination and laboratory assessment in shock, including the role of pulmonary artery catheter data.
